page_content: the subject investigation theory and practicals is essential for police officers as they have to deal with registration and investigation of crimes regularly. in the previous training of sctsis the subjects were investigation theory (paper-a) and investigation practicals and report writing (paper-b) with 165 periods for a paper of 100 marks. the reading material was divided into two books of about 300 pages each with bulky material in two volumes. the trainees had to refer to two books for the same subject and the material was repeated in both books. therefore in the new book for the 2023 batch of sctsis both the subjects have been combined into one and called investigation theory and practicals part i and part ii with 70 periods each in two semesters with 150 marks. part-i in 1st semester includes introduction to investigation, fir, crime scene management, search and seizure, inquest, interview & interrogation, arrest and bail. each topic will be taught in theory followed by practical exercises. this will help the trainee to learn the different aspects of crime investigation and documentation. this book is the result of the tireless work done by smt. c. anasuya, ips, deputy director (induction), in compiling the book with the assistance of sri. venkat rao, ad, tspa. i am sure this book will not only be of immense use to the trainees during the period of training but also as a book of reference in future for investigation. hyderabad date: 24-08-2023 director tspa, hyderabad 1., topics = module-i. 1., topics = introduction to investigation. 1., page no = 1-2. page_content: every human being is vested with personal rights and his corresponding duties towards others. that is, a person has the right of not being harmed by others and at the same time, he has the duty not to harm others. infringement (violation) of one's right is called a wrong. as per law wrongs are classified as civil and criminal in nature. civil cases affect the loss to an individual and the victim in civil case has to approach the court on his own. criminal cases affect the authority of the state to maintain law and order, to protect the lives & properties of its citizens. the state is supposed to conduct investigation on behalf of the citizens and launch criminal prosecution against the offender. hence the police are expected to know the law of the land in detail to exercise their powers in preventing as well as solving a crime. investigation is nothing but collection of facts and circumstances of an incident which has been reported at a police station. investigation begins after a crime has been committed. crime/offence can be a cognizable crime that is non-bailable, or a non-cognizable crime that is bailable. the classification of an offence as to whether it is a cognizable offence, non-cognizable offence, bailable offence or non- bailable offence and compoundable or non-compoundable - is listed in the first schedule of cr.p.c. once fir has been registered by the police authorities, the collection of evidence is mainly in 3 parts: - 1. 2. collecting physical/material evidence 3. recording confessions or statements u/s 164 cr.p.c before the magistrate. 4. filing of charge sheet / final report under section 173 (final report) the powers of police officers to conduct the investigation are dealt under sections 154-176 cr.p.c. 1. he has to receive all information without any discrimination from the complainants and to register cases on the cognizable offences. 2. he has to take-up investigation or to entrust investigation to his subordinate without any delay as per procedure. 3. he has to submit the original fir to the court and copies of fir to the superior officers, immediately after registration the case. 4. he has to collect all the evidence required to establish the offence. 5. he has to produce the arrested accused before the court within 24 hours of his arrest excluding the journey time. 6. he has to collect all the required documents and file along with charge sheet. 1. curiosity: having curiosity involves asking questions to discover the reasons for the occurrence of a particular event. curiosity can help identify new sources to retrieve information for investigation. 2. problem-solving: being a problem solver helps investigators compile the information they gather. a majority of investigation work involves solving a case based on the series of events. 3. research skills: when investigating, it is helpful to know how to collect relevant information from different sources. for instance, an investigator may research the background of perpetrators from official records and previous accomplices to learn about their criminal history etc. 4. evidence collection: evidence collection is the ability to find relevant facts that can help understand why something may have occurred. 5. interviewing skills: interviewing skills are essential to interrogate persons and gather useful information from them. 6. report writing : knowledge of how to prepare a report allows investigators to organise and document the information properly and submit their findings to court to convict or acquit an individual. 7. critical thinking : critical thinking is useful in situations when you have all the information and you need to connect them to arrive at the conclusion. 8. flexibility: to accept changing circumstances, it is essential that investigators are flexible and willing to adapt themselves to new and unexpected situations. 9. observation: another skill necessary to be an investigator is to be attentive to one's surroundings. whether you are observing a suspect, a scene of a crime or a place of interest, your observation skills can help you gather information and infer conclusions faster. ------

page_content: the officer in charge of a police station is vested with the powers to conduct investigation as per cr.p.c. it starts with fir, which sets the criminal law in motion. firs can be broadly classified into four categories; 1. information true in all aspects. 2. information partly true. 3. information true in respect of the offence but not the involvement of the accused cited. 4. information which is totally false. - (a) where no offence has taken place at all and, - (b) where an offence has taken place but the offenders are different from those cited in the fir fir legal provisions : (sections 154,155,156 (3) of criminal procedure code), pso nos.409 to 416) the first information is that information which is given to the police first in point of time relating to the commission of a cognizable case. on receiving the written information in regard to a cognizable offence from the complainant, the sho has to register fir basing on the contents of the information and take up investigation. when information is given orally, it can be written down by any officer on the direction of the officer in charge of a police station. the fir shall be sent forthwith to the magistrate having jurisdiction besides superior police officers (sec. 157 cr.p.c). delay in lodging the fir or in sending it to the magistrate must be satisfactorily explained. otherwise it goes against the prosecution. sometimes information regarding the cognizable offence may be intimated to the officer in charge of a police station by means of a telephonic or telegraphic or sms or e-mail messages. such information does not constitute a valid first information report, since it is not possible to obtain the signature of the informant. if the telephonic or telegraphic/sms/e-mailmessage is given by known person who discloses his identity and it contains all the required facts which can constitute a cognizable offence and is reduced into writing by officer in charge of police station it can be treated as fir. if the sho receives a petition endorsed by the nhrc or shrc or courts or senior officers with instructions to register a case and do investigation on a cognizable offence or non-cognizable offence by the courts, it should be registered. the io should complete investigation and submit final report. if the sho receives a petition or intimation on a cognizable offence from other departments it should be registered as fir. the io should complete investigation and submit final report. suo-moto, meaning on its own motion, is a latin legal term used where a government agency acts on its own cognizance. some times the sho receives information about the occurrence of an illegal act or possession of some prohibited material. he will go to the place and catch the accused, seize the material before the panch witnesses and basing on the panchanama, he will register a case against the accused. this type of fir is called as suo-moto fir. if the sho received information in regard to a cognizable offence through paper news or electronic media reports, he has to make a gd entry and verify the facts and if it is cognizable offence, he has to register a case. as per sec 154 of criminal procedure code, every police officer is law bound to register the first information report of any cognizable offence committed, irrespective of the jurisdiction in which the offence was committed. if it is not committed in his jurisdiction, he registers the fir under number 00 and sends it to the police station, where the offence was committed for further investigation. such fir is called as zero fir. it is for the convenience of the victim that the case is recorded and sent to the concerned police station. a constitution bench of the supreme court in lalita kumari v. govt.

page_content: of u.p [w.p.(crl) no; 68/2008] held that registration of first information report is mandatory under section 154 of the code of criminal procedure if the information discloses commission of a cognizable offence and no preliminary inquiry is permissible in such a situation. if the information received does not disclose a cognizable offence but indicates the necessity for an inquiry, a preliminary inquiry may be conducted only to ascertain whether cognizable offence is disclosed or not. - (i) the police officer cannot avoid his duty of registering offence if cognizable offence is disclosed. action must be taken against erring officers who do not register the fir if information received by them discloses a cognizable offence. - (ii) a preliminary inquiry should not exceed 7 days. the fact of such delay and the causes of it must be reflected in the general diary entry. procedure to be followed in case of information received in police station about the commission of a non-cognizable offence there are two procedures: - whenever sho receives a petition/complaint containing the contents of offence non-cognizable in nature, should make entry in the general diary mentioning the contents of the petition in brief. - sho should explain to the complainant that police cannot investigate a case non-cognizable in nature and he has to seek - redressal in court of law. - sho must inform the same in writing to the petitioner. procedure to be followed to obtain permission from the court to taking up investigation in case sho receives petition/complaint containing the contents of non-cognizable offence. - the sho should make entry in the general diary mentioning the contents of the petition in brief. - he will make an endorsement on the original petition. - mentioning the date and time of receipt of petition - also mention about the commission of offences under noncognizable sections of offence (mention the sections specifically). - and also write on the petition that the magistrate concerned is being requested to accord permission to investigate the case as per the provisions of section 155(2) cr.p.c. - prepare a report addressing the area judicial magistrate under section 155(2) cr.p.c. for according permission to investigate the information relating to non-cognizable offence and send it to court by enclosing copy of the petition. - sho has to make entry in the gd about the receipt of proceedings. - sho should endorse on the original petition mentioning the date and time of receipt of proceedings of the magistrate. - and also mention case crime no. and section of law and taking up the investigation. - sho has to issue fir on the original petition by allotting the regular crime no. - (a) write the fir immediately with all available details in the report/petition, and enter the brief of fact at once in the gd - (b) name of the complainant/informant, father's/husband's name, age, occupation and residence should be noted. - (c) the time of reporting at the police station and brief particulars of the report and the crime number and section of law and indicating the action taken. - (d) obtain the signature (if illiterate, the thumb impression) of the complainant or informant on all the pages of the statement. - (e) record on the original written statement of the informant, the date and hour of its receipt and through whom it was received. - (f) ascertain and incorporate in the fir the reasons for the delay, if any, in the receipt of the report or in the making of the complaint at the police station. - (g) place of occurrence and distance and direction from the police station. - (h) name and address of the accused (the full name of each accused with aliases, if any, address and father's name) - (i) details of property stolen/taken away, if any. if the list of stolen property is lengthy, it must be made on a separate sheet of a paper and attached to the fir and fact mentioned in this column with signatures of the both the complainant and the sho. in simple the complaint / report must cover the following 5'w's & 1 'h'.

page_content: - 1- w - what happened? - 2- w - when did it happen? - 3- w - where did it happen? - 4- w - who possibly committed the crime? - 5- w - why it happened (motive) - 1- h - how was it committed? the sho of a police station not below the rank of a constable can register the fir. he may be - (a) head constable - (b) asst. sub-inspector - (c) sub-inspector - (d) inspector - (e) deputy superintendent of police or other senior officer. the sho has to register the case basing on the contents of report / complainant under appropriate sections of law. if there is any doubt he has to take advice from the senior officers or from the prosecuting officers. if case is not registered under appropriate section of law it may cause difficulty in investigation. thus the sho has to take care while registering the case. if delay has occurred in lodging the information by the informant or victim, the officer investigating the case should obtain explanation from the informant in regard to such delay and incorporate the same in the statement of the witnesses. the firs/crimes having gravity in nature are called as grave firs / crimes. these cases are to investigated by the inspector rank officer or above. in general these crimes are serious in nature and may affect the public peace and tranquility. the following classes of cases will be termed as grave firs/ crimes : - (a) dacoity - (b) high-way/house/ train robbery - (c) robbery over rs.1000/- - (d) murder - (e) all terrorist offences and those affecting security - (f) culpable homicide not amounting to murder - (g) rape - (h) house breaking and theft over rs.50, 000/- - (i) any disturbance, riot or affray of a specially grave nature - (j) theft over rs.1,00,000/- - (k) cases of counterfeit coins and currency notes - (l) organized crime of any description including kidnapping / abduction for ransom. - (m) cases of manufacture or use of bombs or explosives or arms and ammunition whether political or not - (n) assault on police officers, magistrates or judicial officers - (o) offences under sections 400 & 401 of the indian penal code - (p) conspiracy cases - (q) serious road accidents - (r) professional poisoning and drug trafficking, death or injury by noxious gases, cases arising due to food poisoning or consumption of adulterated liquor or spurious drugs. - (s) offences resulting in grievous hurt and serious mischief or arson committed on members of scs & sts on caste consideration - (t) attempted suicide or death in suspicious circumstances by young married women during the first 7 years of their marriage, trafficking in women and children - (u) cases of misappropriation involving property of value of rs.2 lakhs or more of government department or undertakings or financial institutions - banking or non-banking. - (v) major organized financial crimes affecting public and involving public money of over rs. 5 lakhs value the grave crimes or other crimes which are having seriousness and disturb the peace and tranquility of large number of people are called as especially grave fir / crime. the sdpo / acp or above the rank of sdpo / acp officer has to do investigation in these cases and to send immediate report to the government. the following offences are called as specially grave firs / crimes: - (a) dacoity - (b) highway robbery (all robberies of railway passengers are regarded as highway robberies) - (c) murder of a sensational nature - (d) manufacture of counterfeit currency notes and coins - (e) trafficking in drugs (narcotics and psychotropic substances) and drug poisoning cases - (f) communal riots - (g) caste riots

page_content: - (h) atrocities involving arson, murder, rape, assault or molestation against scheduled castes and scheduled tribes - (i) disturbance, riot or affray of a specially grave nature - (j) disturbance involving the use of fire arms and or explosives - (k) misappropriation of public funds and property of value of rs. 10 lakhs and above - (l) case in which fire is opened by a public servant - (m) organized large-scale misappropriation, criminal breach of trust, cheating by bogus institutions, finance companies etc. - (n) organized crime (mafia type or syndicated crime) including kidnapping for ransom, gang wars etc. - (o) case of possession, use or manufacture of bombs or explosive devices - (p) abduction or kidnapping or trafficking in women and children in an organised manner - (q) assault on the police except of a very trivial nature - (r) case of death or grievous injury allegedly by a police officer whether in public or private capacity, or to have occurred to any person (including suicide) while in police custody, or alleged police torture. - (s) serious fire accident involving loss of properties worth rs.10 lakhs and above, or involving loss of human life or both. - (t) fast, hartal, strike, bandh, or any form of agitation, which attracts public attention, or disrupts normal life or movement of traffic or accompanied by violence. - (u) serious accident or natural calamity in which human lives are involved - (v) railway accident except where it is trivial - (w) terrorist violence - (x) case of sensational nature or of public importance the sho/sub-inspector of police has to inform about the occurrence of the grave or especially grave crime/fir as soon as possible to the superior officers by quickest means and to take precautions to control the law & order situation in the area. the sho has to forward the grave crime fir/ especially grave crime fir to the court, superintendent of police/cp and to the district magistrate in later case with special messenger. the copy of the fir should be dispatched or made available to the following officers by the earliest possible means: - the area judicial magistrate (in original). - the district collector - superintendent of police - nhai (national high way authority of india) in case of grave accidents - dcrb (district crime record bureau) - sub divisional police officer - station file - the complainant or informant (free copy). after registration of fir the sho has to mention the information in the records of police station. these records are two categories. one is bodily offence records and second is property offence records. 1. general diary 2. fir index / book 3. sch part-i 4. sch part-iv ( village history sheet) 5. rowdy sheet (if the accused is having rowdy sheet) etc. 1. general diary 2. fir index/book 3. sch part - i 4. sch part - ii (jurisdiction map) 5. sch part - iv (village history sheet) 6. dc/kd/suspect sheet (if the offender is having one) 7. lli (loose leaf index) etc. a first information report book / index shall be maintained in form 96 (pso-no-630). the entries in the register should be continued from year to year till all the pages in a book are used. the classification as per instructions relating to part-i of station crime history should be entered in this register in cases where it is not entered in part-i. even though no separate columns are provided in this register the entries should be made in the remarks column. all cognizable offences and reports forwarded by courts on endorsement including non-cognizable cases shall be registered in the fir book. the information pertaining to following may also be registered in the pro-forma of the fir. however, the offences so registered will not be given fir number, but details shall be recorded in special registers maintained for this purpose.

page_content: all cases of suicides and accidental deaths where inquest is conducted under section 174(3) cr.p.c.,accidental fires, missing of persons, missing cattle, etc, cases booked under sections 41, 107 to 110 and 102 cr.p.c. if the report is made at police outpost, the incharge shall record the statement of the informant and send it immediately to the police station for the registration after entering the substance in the outpost g.d. however if a written report is presented its gist will be entered in the outpost g.d. and the report will be sent to the police station for immediate registration. if the officer in charge of police station receives a report during his tour, he should send it to the station with due endorsement for registration. in the meanwhile he can commence investigation. it is not necessary that a formal registration of a case should have been made before proceeding to the spot, in order to bring inquest proceedings within the ambit of investigation. any step taken by a police officer, pursuant to such information, towards detection of the said offence, would be part of such investigation, even though the formal registration of the fir takes place only thereafter. the object of inquest is only to ascertain whether a person has died under unnatural circumstances and if so, what the cause of death is, ( sambu das vs. st. of assam air 2010 sc 3300, st. of up vs. bhagwant kishore joshi air 1964 sc 221 , maha singh vs. state (delhi administration) air 1976 sc 449) . the element of delay in registering fir or sending fir to the magistrate by would not in any manner weaken the prosecution case. ( balram singh vs state of punjab air 2003 sc 2213 ). where the fir contained only a brief statement of events, the delay in sending the fir to court could not have been to concoct a false case against the accused. in hallu and others v state of madhya pradesh , it is held that it is not the requirement of section 154 cr.p.c which speaks of an information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence given to an in-charge of a police station that the report must be given by a person who has knowledge of the incident and such report cannot be refused to be treated as f.i.r holding that it is based on hearsay evidence. in golla jalla reddy v state of ap , it has been held that where oral complaint disclosing commission of a cognizable offence has been made and police investigation has started on its basis and later on a second report is made in writing, the written report has to be considered as statement under section 161 cr.p.c and not as f.i.r and it cannot be used for corroboration of evidence of information. information received in regard to commission of a cognizable offence is not required to be preceded by a f.i.r. it is the duty of the state to protect the life of the injured. intimation of accidents received from a medical officer can be treated as fir. (1968 m.l.j. (cr) 75 ap) an information given by a constable to the si at his house that an injured person is in a precarious condition and was taken to the hospital will have to be treated as the fir (1965 (2) cr.l.j. 762 guj.) an fir is a public document. however, it is not substantive evidence. it can be used only to corroborate or contradict the informant. ------

page_content: the place of occurrence of the crime where physical evidences and clues relating to the crime are available is called scene of offence/crime scene. it may be any place either indoor, outdoor or a vehicle which provides information on the victim and the suspect, and is used to reconstruct the crime. as the part of the investigation the investigating officer should immediately reach the scene of crime, identify the available physical evidences and collect them. basing on the nature of crime, clues team must be called in to collect physical evidences if necessary. any delay to collect physical evidences, leads to the (wiping out) of them and it will be very difficult for experts to give their opinion. it helps to establish corpus delicti (latin term meaning 'body of the crime'), linkage between the criminal, the victim and the scene of occurrence through exchange of clues. physical location of the crime scene is the place where a crime has been committed and where physical evidence or clues of such a crime are found. there may be one or more crime scenes. in addition to the original crime scene the area to an extent of 3 to 4 kms surrounding it must be searched for any objects or articles left by criminals or hidden stolen property with the help of dog squad. the io should observe the type of crime at the crime scene. there may be certain types of crime, which have no 'scene' at all. the io should observe whether the scene of offence is located indoor or outdoor and guard it. he should observe it thoroughly with the help of clues team, and collect the physical evidence like fingerprints, footprints, blood stains, threads or any other clues, left by the criminals on the scene. the scene of crime is not confined within the limits of immediate location. it can extend to as much of its surrounding as per the circumstances for the commission of the crime. it includes the way of approach and retreat of the culprit. sometimes same crime may have both indoor and outdoor scene. for example in a murder scene there may be multiple crime scenes: - (i) the place from where the person was kidnapped. - (ii) the place where he/she was confined. - (iii) the place where the initial assault commenced leading to death. - (iv) the place where the dead body was moved. - (v) the place where physical or trace evidence or parts of the body connected with the crime are found. - (vi) the vehicle used to transport the dead body. the scene of offence must be observed in a proper way before commencing investigation. it enables to know the modus operandi of the crime. the crime scenes are classified broadly in to three types basing on the place of occurrence: the outdoor crime scene is the most vulnerable to loss, contamination, and deleterious change of physical evidence in a relatively short period of time. destruction or deterioration of evidence due to environmental conditions such as heat, cold, rain, and wind are problems associated with outdoor scenes. investigators who encounter a combination of an indoor and outdoor scene should give priority to processing the outdoor component. night time outdoor crime scenes are particularly problematic. whenever possible, outdoor crime scenes should be held and secured until daylight for processing. evidence at an indoor scene is generally less susceptible to loss, contamination and deleterious change. indoor crime scenes are usually easier to secure and protect, and securing a scene can be as simple as closing a door. conveyance is defined as 'something that serves as a means of transportation.' it is important that the i.o. recognizes that physical evidence recovered from these scenes may extend well beyond the conveyance itself. for example, impression evidence, such as shoe or footprints in soil, may be found leading away from the scene, and property removed from the conveyance may be deposited or dropped as the perpetrator flees the scene. it is not difficult to locate the scene of occurrence, but sometimes criminals simulate a scene to mislead the investigators, to avoid suspicion and try to pass on the guilt to others, sometimes natural phenomena or stray animals may alter the scene of occurrence.

page_content: crime scenes are further classifies in to below mentioned four types based on the number of locations of crime occurrence . - (i) primary crime scene - (ii) secondary crime scene - (iii) tertiary crime scene - (iv) continuing crime scene - (i) primary crime scene: the original location of a crime or accident where a crime actually took place. sometimes the crime scene may extend to another crime scene. investigating officer should visit the secondary scene. - (ii) secondary crime scene: secondary crime scene means that actual crime took place at one point and continued in other place where the crime was finally committed by the offenders, an alternate location where additional evidence may be found. the crime scene includes the victim's home, suspect's vehicle and road between the suspect and victim locations. an offender enters a residence (primary crime scene) takes the daughter in another room to sexually assault her (secondary crime scene) transport the victim (car would be the tertiary crime scene) commit another crime at a distant location for instance murder and dump the body (fourth crime scene). - (iii) tertiary crime scene: tertiary crime scene means the scene where the offence was committed after primary and secondary scenes. ex. a person was kidnapped in primary scene, murdered in secondary scene and buried in another place. it is called as tertiary crime scene. - (iv) continuing crime scene: the crimes may have been committed and completed at a number of places. all the sites are crimes scenes, where evidence relating to the crime is found. this is called as continuing crime scene. - (a) investigating officer: it is important that the investigating officer and his team proceed to the scene of crime as expeditiously as possible. io looks at the scene of crime to find out how the crime was committed, the physical clues that are to be collected, packed and transported and the experts to be contacted or called to the scene of offence, what method of search to be employed for the collection of physical clues etc. - (b) forensic experts: the forensic scientist has special training and education in chemistry and biology that is applied to the recognition, identification, collection, and preservation of physical evidence. various materials collected from the scene or other places connected to the crime are sent to concerned experts for their opinion. a forensic scientist collects evidence, biological evidence etc., and does crime scene reconstruction. (section 45 iea). - (c) clues team / fpb: clues team works under the technical supervision of the director of tsfsl. similarly, it will function under the direct supervision of the sp/cp concerned.

page_content: - c - crime l -laboratories u -ultimatum e - evidence s - system. one vehicle is provided exclusively for clues team, for its mobility with all scientific instruments for collection, observation and recording of physical evidences. 1. technician 2. photographer/ videographer 3. finger prints expert 4. one home guard for assistance. - (i) collection of physical evidence. - (ii) recording the scene of offence (photograph and video graph). - (iii) proper packing and handing over of physical evidences to i.o. dogs are used not only in the investigation of crimes, but also, to identify hashish (ganja), explosives and landmines in airports, railway stations and in the meeting places of national leaders. dog can easily smell any scent, and has the capacity to resolve the secretions of sweat glands, of human beings, even in minute quantity i.e. microgram level. it can smell the scent of a substance buried under a depth of 5 feet. dogs are also used for identification of human beings. the clues given by dog must be briefly taken into consideration, for the purpose of investigation. but, it cannot be proved absolutely, that a person is a criminal, basing on the evidence provided by dog. the i.o must treat each and every act of the dog as a clue and treat the dog as informant, collect sufficient evidence, combine it with other evidences of experts and produce in court to prove the crime. broadly there are three (3) basic and simple stages in properly processing the crime scene.those stages consist of 1) scene recognition 2) scene documentation and 3) evidence collection. proper methods, techniques, knowledge of resources and equipment, all are pertinent to evidence collection. proper handling and packaging of evidence by the io / expert are also important. each crime scene is different and may require a different approach to processing the scene. however there is a basic crime scene protocol that should be adhered to in all crime scenes. the protocol will be of five steps. interview is the first step in processing a crime scene. io may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case. an interview is a conversation between the police men and a suspect/accused/complainant and witness or victim which is controlled somewhat by the police officers.

page_content: examine the crime scene as the second step in the protocol. examine the scene of offence to identify the possible items of evidence. identify point of entry and point of exit, and get the general layout of the crime scene. the third step in the protocol involves creating a pictorial record of the scene as well as a rough sketch to demonstrate the layout of the crime scene and to identify the exact position of the deceased or other evidence within the crime scene. in the documentation stage there are simple steps to properly document the crime scene. sketching the scene, photography, observation notes and videography. 1. protection 2. photography 3. videography 4. drawing the sketch of the soc 5. drawing observation mahazar 6. lifting of finger and foot prints and other impressions 7. examination by forensic science experts. 8. recording statements of witness at the soc preservation of the clue in the crime scene is the most important task of the police. he should isolate the area of the crime by cordoning it off. suitable containers like polythene or glass bottles, polythene bags, paper envelopes, aluminum foils, etc. and preservative substances should be kept ready for proper preservation of evidence. this is the last step in the protocol. if the accused / suspect is found at the crime scene, he should be examined thoroughly to elicit evidence in regard to the motive of crime, names of accused, weapon used, manner in which the offence was committed. his confession should be recorded before mediators and the crime weapons etc seized. the primary purpose of inspecting the scene of crime is to search for evidence left behind by the criminal while committing the crime, known as burglars' visiting cards. such evidence will be invariably present in all scenes of crime is based on the locard's principle of exchange. the search is conducted following a spiral pattern working from outside to inside towards the center or focal point (towards the body, safe or any other key feature) or vice versa, either clockwise or anti clockwise. it is an effective search pattern for single person search, with a very high probability of locating minute evidence like fingerprints, footprints, etc. also, there is not much likelihood of the evidence being destroyed by the investigator's movements. this method is recommended for indoor scenes of crime. it can also conduct outdoor scenes of crime if the area to be searched is small. here the i.o makes an ever-widening circular movement in which he starts at the focal point of the scene. the search is conducted in a spiral pattern working from outside to inside towards the center or focal point vice versa either clockwise or anti clockwise.

page_content: grid method is a slight modification of the strip method. this method is useful for small areas especially indoors. the area is divided into grids. in this method a single person can search the entire area by moving at right angles, and covering all the squares of the grid. grid method wheel search this method of search is employed to inspect large outdoor scenes. the entire area is covered by a circle. the searchers start from the centre and proceed along the radial. they may have to repeat the process several times depending on the size of the circle. commonly used when searching a large area looking for a large object. several explorers will stand in one long line and all walk the same direction. stakes and string can also be used to create 'lanes' for which each explorer would be responsible. if the crime scene is large and enough personnel are not available, it is advisable to divide or sub-divide the area into smaller zones and sub zones and use this method of search. the method is particularly useful for searching in details the enclosed areas where items such as almirahs, safes, etc. have to be carefully inspected, and for cars or carriages which may contain minute trace evidence, necessitating a thorough search of all nooks and corners. zone method there are three methods of drawing of crime scene sketch in crime investigation. the io has to use the method basing on the nature of the crime scene. - (a) coordinate method or baseline method : this method uses the principle of measuring the distance of an object from two fixed points. the fixed points are normally selected in such a way that they are straight and most of the evidence is located in the vicinity. the line joining these points is known as the baseline. in indoor scenes one side of a wall of the room where the crime occurred can be taken as the baseline. in outdoor scenes any two trees or other fixed points can be joined and taken as the baseline.

page_content: baseline or coordinate method - (b) triangulation method : this method is particularly suitable for outdoor locations. two or more fixed points should be located and they should be as widely separated as possible. the evidentiary item need not be very close to the fixed point but should be located by measuring along a straight line from the fixed points. - (c) cross-projection method : it is useful when the evidentiary items are on the ceiling, wall or elsewhere in an enclosed space. the walls, windows and doors in a cross projection sketch are drawn as though the walls are extended flat on the floor. the measurements are taken from any two wall corners or roof corners to the evidentiary item. triangulation method cross projection method the io has to prepare the sketch of crime scene basing on the place of occurrence by using any one of the sketch methods explained above. sketches are of two types: - (1) rough sketch of the scene of crime - (2) sketching the crime scene on scale the sketch is the first visual record of the condition of the crime scene. it portrays the crime scene and items within that scene that are of interest to the investigation. sketches properly prepared are useful during the interrogation of witnesses, in making notes in the case diaries and in presenting information to the court. the sketch complements the photographs and notes made while observing the crime scene. some model sketchs of crime are given below. bsapp.com the investigating officer should make a rough sketch of the crime scene on the basis of his own observation. the sketch need not be drawn to scale, but should indicate accurate distances between objects, dimensions of areas and relative directions.

page_content: the items to be included in the sketch and the number of copies should be determined at the outset. all sketches should cover the following aspects in the case of indoor scenes, the size of the window(s) and room(s) and in the case of outdoors the scene with reference to 'magnetic north'. the apparently visible items should be indicated by accurate measurement from at least two fixed points. the position of eyewitnesses and accused persons and places indicating all stains, footprints, tire or drag marks and other objects of victim or accused. the following information must invariably be mentioned on the sketch. - the 'investigator's full name, rank, address. - crime number, section of law, police station, and district - address of the crime scene, its position in a building, landmarks and compass direction - the visible items of physical evidence & critical features of the crime scene. - a legend to the symbols may be used to identify objects or points. the rough sketch once drawn should not be altered. a smooth or fair sketch should be prepared on the basis of the rough sketch. the smooth sketch also known as crime scene plan drawing is prepared as per a convenient scale and plan from the details of rough sketch. color drawing will be helpful to distinguish various objects or the features of an object. photography is admissible in evidence u/s 9 of the evidence act. photos of various physical evidences present in the scene of offence must be taken in sequence, to know how the crime was committed. a camera records all details accurately which may escape the mind. in all-important cases, photographs, video graphs and where possible laser impressions should be taken of the scene and of the objects on which any useful clues are found. - (i) entire scene of offence with permanent structures of that area must be photographed. - (ii) if the injuries on a dead body are convenient for identification, photograph must be taken along with the place of dead body in such a way as to identify the injuries. - (iii) the physical objects like weapons must be photo graphed along with their place of location. - (iv) finger prints, foot prints and signs of tools, must be photographed. - (v) in various cases, surroundings of the scene of offence must also be covere in photo. note: the name and address of the photographer, description of the camera, date and time of photography and condition of the light at that time must be mentioned in case diary and photos should be sent to court. it is valuable for showing an overview of the crime scene and should be considered in major cases. it refreshes the memory of those who were involved in processing the crime scene. video tapes are considered evidence. in certain crimes like arson, videography provides an overall view of the scene and allows investigators who could not be present at the scene to visualize the important pieces of evidence and their relationship to one another. all the precautions suggested earlier for photography should also be followed for these recordings.

page_content: the officer making the search should take down accurate and detailed notes, supported by accurate sketches drawn to scale, showing the whole layout and the exact places where the articles, etc. were found. it is not sufficient to say that an article was in a certain room or on a particular table, but its exact position must be noted and, if necessary, an enlarged sketch of that portion of the scene must be drawn. note making of crime scene: guidelines for recording observation notes: - (i) method of recording : the notes should start with the time of receipt of first information. the notes can be recorded as and when the observations are made. - (ii) date, time and location : the time and date of arrival at crime scene and the details of its location, environmental conditions (smoke, rain, dusk, dawn, etc.) the names of persons present at the scene on arrival should be recorded. - (iii) description of the victim and clothing : the name, age, height, weight, complexion, color of hair, eyes, if possible, the date of birth of victim should be noted. outer garments should be described in terms of type of garment and colour. - (iv) wounds on the victim : the exact location of a wound or injury, its type, size and in the case of a bruise, its color. - (v) recognition of each significant item of evidence : the description of each significant piece of evidence found or identified with complete details, such as the time when it was found, the exact location, who found it, how it was marked, sealed and packed. - (vi) missing items : the purpose of this entry is to note items that should have been normally associated with the crime being investigated, but which are absent at the crime scene or on the victim or deceased. for example, an item of clothing missing from the victim's body that could not be located at the scene of crime and which, if found later from the suspect or elsewhere, should be useful in linking evidence and suspect or vice-versa. - (vii) miscellaneous : any other details not included in photographs and sketches such as condition of surroundings, the investigating officer can reorganize all these notes for preparing his case diary and charge sheet. panchanama should be written to record the scene of offence. it should be written in the presence of local respectable person. names of respectable person should be mentioned. distance and direction of the place of writing panchanama from the ps, crime number, and section of law, police station and name of the investigation officer along with other present police officers must be mentioned in this panchanama. these proceedings must be sent to the court. crime scene reconstruction is the process of determining or eliminating the events and actions that occurred at the crime scene. analysis of the crime scene pattern, the location and position of the physical evidence and the laboratory examination of the physical evidence involves systematic study of related information and the logical formulation of a theory. reconstruction is considered a scientific fact-gathering process. the following are the five separate stages commonly used in the process of reconstruction. 1. data collection 2. conjecture 3. hypothesis formulation 4. testing 5. theory formation -------

page_content: evidence used to resolve a crime is of two types: oral evidence and physical evidence. they are available in the scene of offence, with the criminal and victim: 1. body fluids: blood, semen, saliva, sweat, tears, urine and feaces 2. parts of the body: skull, flesh, teeth, bones, nails, intestines and hair. 3. physical and chemical toxic substances and their containers. 4. documents and photos, video clippings, pens and inks. 5. guns, explosives and their leftovers. 6. cotton and woolen threads. 7. finger prints, foot prints, shoe prints. 8. glass pieces, dry paint chips. 9. signs of machines: signs of type writer, tyre marks. 10. signs of teeth and lips. 11. signs carved on metal articles: serial numbers. 12. soil and dust (occupational dust). 13. signs of tools. 14. implements: blades, screw driver, hammer, crowbar, spade, knives, dragons, bow and arrows. 15. substances related to plants & animals. apart from the above, any other material, which shows link between the criminal and scene of offence, maybe utilized as physical evidence. trace evidence is invariably small in quantity whenever two objects come into mutual contact, each leaves trace evidence on the other. this trace may be distinct, faint or invisible depending on the nature of the object and place. whenever two people come in contact with each other, a physical transfer occurs. hair, skin, clothing fibers, pollen, glass fragments, any number of different types of material can be transferred from one person to another. these transferred materials constitute what is called trace evidence. small but measurable amounts of physical or biological material may be found at a crime scene. the scene of offence must be preserved carefully until the arrival of finger prints and explosives and clues team members. physical evidence should be described in detail with full information as to when and where such evidence was found who found it, handled it and how it was disposed. each item should be packed separately so as to avoid breakage, loss or contamination in transit. tweezers, forceps and similar tools are used to collect and place traces and small items into containers. documentary evidence should be first placed in transparent envelopes without folding or bending, and then placed between two pieces of rigid cardboard and packed in a suitable cloth-lined envelope cover. if the stain is on a solid object that can be moved, such as a knife, crowbar, fire arm or any other weapon, it is preferable to transport the whole object, protecting the area of the stain or completely enclosing the object in a package (if it is small enough). the specific areas of such fabric which contain trace evidence should be folded in such a way as to protect such areas to avoid loss of such materials by falling off, peeling off, etc. food and edible substances should be placed in as many separate moisture proof bottles or containers as necessary to avoid any contamination. while lifting and packing materials for dispatch to the fsl meticulous care should be taken to see that no article is inadvertently contaminated with extraneous matter or things likely to interfere in its examination. physical evidence available in the scene should be collected in the presence of elderly persons of that area under a mahazar. integrity of the evidence, i.e original character of the evidence should be maintained while collecting it. the chain of custody of evidence collected should be recorded in detail. each item should be packed separately so as to avoid breakage, loss or contamination in transit. the term chain of custody means maintaining a record of every article of evidence, from the point of discovery at a crime scene to its collection and transport to the laboratory for examination and its temporary storage till it is finally submitted to the court. chain of custody assures continuous accountability. failure to substantiate the chain of custody may lead to serious questions regarding the authenticity and integrity of the evidence and the examination rendered upon it, making the whole process an exercise in futility.

page_content: the io has to forward the material collected from the scene of offence, victim and accued to the expert for opinion through a letter with proper authorisation. in the proforma prescribed for this purpose. this proforma is called letter of advice in form 54. the forwarding letter should be sent in a separate sealed cover either by a special messenger or by a post. the following information / particulars must be mentioned, in the letter of advice:- - (i) police station, crime number, section of law. - (ii) number of persons or animals. - (iii) how many died. - (iv) history of the case in brief, reasons for the case, when, how and where. - (v) list of the articles sent for examination, their particulars, from where and what circumstances they were seized should be mentioned. the serial number of the list and the label serial number must tally. - (vi) nature of examination must be mentioned for each item. - (vii) method of packing, name and number of the messenger constable should be mentioned. - (viii) samples seal - seals on the wooden box and inner packet must be similar. wax or lac, must be applied on the seals. any police officer, who is conducting investigation, may examine orally any witness, who is acquainted with the facts of the case. the examination of a witness and recording his statement by a police officer during the course of investigation dealt with the sections 160,161,162,163,154 and 171 of the cr.p.c. the competency and examination of a witness is dealt in sec. 118 to 134 of iea. the examination of witness in criminal case by a police officer is an important task which must be done diligently and intelligently. in important cases judicial magistrate is competent to record statement under section 164 cr.p.c. if an accused person, on being arrested, expresses his willingness to make a confession, his confession may be recorded under section 164 of cr.p.c by the competent magistrate. complainant is a person who gives first information about a crime and gives the scope to the police to do justice. the information given by the complainant must be written in the words of complainant. accused may also be examined according to sec 161 of cr.p.c. it is important and necessary for an investigating officer to record the statement of an accused person in complicated cases. the investigating officer should secure the presence of two respectable and independent witnesses of the locality and in their presence record the confessional statement. a person who directly witnesses the incident is called eye witness and his statement should record under section 161 cr.p.c. the statement of each witness should be recorded separately, in direct form and as far as possible in his own language and with the full version of what he states. all-important witnesses should be questioned at the scene of offence because their memory reacts better that way. the investigation officer may by an order in writing, require the attendance before himself of any person who appears to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case and such person shall attend as so required. the io should arrange to be made for his return to the dwelling place and he may not be made to spend his money to reach home. the investigation officer is not vested with powers to require the attendance of a woman of any age and a male who is below 15 yrs of age or elderly persons before him for examining her/him as witness. he shall go to the place of residence of or choice of the victim or witness of such woman or male and examine and record the statement there itself.

page_content: in some cases witnesses may be produced before judicial magistrate competent to record such statements under sec-164 cr.p.c. in some cases accused person on being arrested expresses his willingness to make a confession. his confession may be got recorded u/s 164 cr.p.c. by the competent magistrate. complainant comes to the police station with injuries, he should be sent to hospital immediately and statement can be recorded in the hospital. any police officer not below the rank of an inspector may be permitted enter the jail to interview the prisoners on a letter of authority to the superintendent of jails from sp as per the prisoners act. a confession made by an accused is irrelevant in a criminal proceeding, if the making of the confession appears to the court to have been caused by any inducement, threat or promise, having reference to the charge against the accused person. no confession made to a police officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence. no confession made by any person whilst he is in the custody of a police officer, unless it is made in the immediate presence of a magistrate shall be proved as against such person. the statement is given by an accused to a police officer while in custody which reveals the discovery of any material fact and in consequence of that statement if that material fact is discovered, that statement is admissible to the extent of such discovery. s.o. 431-1. in important cases, witnesses may be produced before judicial magistrate competent to record such statements under section 164 cr.p.c. such statements can be used to contradict the statements given by the same person during the course of enquiry or trial. if he retracts later he can be prosecuted for giving false evidence. under the telangana control of organized crime act 2001 and prevention of terrorism act (pota) 2002, confessional statements made to police officer not below the rank of the superintendent of police and recorded by such police officer either in writing or on any mechanical devices can be admissible in the trial of such person or co-accused, abettor or conspirator provided that, the co-accused, abettor or conspirator is charged and tried in the same case together with the accused. the confession should be recorded in a free atmosphere in the same language in which the person is examined and as narrated by him. (so. 433-1) 1. as soon as it appears to the investigating officer that an accused is likely to make a confession leading to the discovery of a fact, the investigating officer should secure the presence of two respectable and independent witnesses of the locality and in their presence record the confessional statement, obtaining the signatures of the witnesses on the record. 2. the statement of the accused should be in the first person and in the very words of the accused. 3. when a discovery is made as the result of the statement of the accused at another place, a separate panchanama should be drawn up for the discovery. 4. giving information and recovery that follows it are two different transactions and a separate panchanama should be recorded for each of 5. them. 5. the information given by an accused person should not be mixed up in the panchanama drawn up for the recovery made in consequence 7. of such information. 6. but if other accused persons suspected to have taken part in burying the property at the place also give the same information subsequently the discovery cannot be attributed to second person. 7. these confessional statements cannot be said to have led to the discovery of the property which has already been discovered and are not, therefore, relevant under that section. 8. the accused must be in custody of police for a confession to come under purview of section 27 of evidence act. 9. even a man released on bail and giving information leading to discovery of a fact can be deemed to be in police custody within meaning of section 27. 10. arrest and custody are not synonymous. a person can be in custody without being formally arrested.

page_content: extra judicial confession is one which is made outside the court of judicial proceedings. ex; a confession made to a private person. a confession made to a magistrate immediately after commission of an offence and before registration of fir. dying declaration is evidence given by a person before his death. it has got very important evidentiary value in the court of law. section 32 (1) of the i.e.a explains about the dying declaration. normally if a person is admitted in the hospital with severe injuries and is on death bed, doctor will send intimation to police to record his statement as well as intimation to the judicial magistrate to record his dying declaration. after receipt of the intimation, i.o goes to the hospital, records the statement of the injured and if necessary, he also sends requisition to the magistrate to record the dying declaration. in emergency conditions, anybody including doctor and police can record dying declaration, but the d.d record by the judicial magistrate has more evidentiary value over the d.d. recorded by others. if the court believes the d.d, it can convict the accused based only on its evidentiary value. but, in normal circumstances d.d requires corroborative evidence or circumstantial evidence to prove the guilt of the accused. while recording the d.d of a person, he should be in full senses and depose evidence voluntarily and not under the influence of others. hence, it is better to obtain a certificate from the attending doctor on the d.d itself, stating that the injured is in conscious state and is in a position to depose evidence. during the trial of the case the judicial magistrate may be examined as witness. the statement of a woman against whom an offence under section 354, a,b,c,d, section 376, a,ab,b,c,d,da,db,e, or section 509 of the indian penal code is alleged to have been committed or attempted, shall be recorded, by a woman police officer or any woman officer. they should be examined at their residence and preferable in the company of another woman (women pc). they cannot be summoned to the police station for recording of statements. the statement of the child shall be recorded at the residence of the child or at a place where he usually resides or at the place of his choice and as far as practicable by a woman police officer not below the rank of sub-inspector. the police officer while recording the statement of the child shall not be in uniform. the judicial magistrate shall record the statement of the person against whom such offence has been committed in the manner prescribed in subsection (5), as soon as the commission of the offence is brought to the notice of the police. the women victim should be dealt with utmost sympathy and sensitivity. behavior towards the victim should be extremely courteous. no embarrassing or indecent questions should be put to the victim. a child is competent witness u/s 118 of the evidence act. child witness should be handled with care and affection. leading questions should be avoided. the advantage of child witness is that their observation is keen and truth is only likely to come as they are innocent and are not carried by ill-conceived notions. the statement of the child shall be recorded at the residence of the child at a place where he usually resides. the police officer making the investigation, shall, while examining the child, ensure that at no point of time the child comes in the contact in any way with the accused. the io may take the assistance of a translator or an interpreter, having such qualifications, experience and on payment of such fees as may be prescribed, while recording the statement of the child. when a case is transferred from one investigating officer should another, the officer taking over the investigation shall take charge of the diary, noting therein the date of assuming charge of the investigation of the case. succeeding investigating officer to re-examine all the important witnesses already examined, to ascertain the facts and the circumstances of the case. if any additional facts are spoken to by any witness, the second io will have to record those facts and note such other facts as may be necessary in clarification of the facts and omissions in the statement already recorded. the magistrate or the police officer, as the case may be, may, in the case of a child having a mental or physical disability, seek the assistance of a special educator or any person familiar with the manner of communication of the child or an expert in that field, having such qualifications, experience and on payment of such fees as may be prescribed, to record the statement of the child. wherever possible, the magistrate or the police officer, as the case may be, shall ensure that the statement of the child is also recorded by audio-video electronic means. further in some important cases like pocso act and sc & st poa act the statements of the victim and witnesses are to be recorded under videography. ------

page_content: the direct evidence, which is documentary in nature, may be either primary evidence or secondary evidence. primary evidence may be proved directly in the court by filing the original document itself. if the original is lost, the carbon or xerox copy of the document intended to be proved may be filed and it is called as secondary evidence. documents play an important role in investigation. roughly 70% of physical evidence relates to disputed documents therefore the io should understand the various types of questioned documents, the problems of documents examination, the care and preservation of documents and the assistance he can obtain from document experts. in general there are three kinds of documents / writings. they are admitted, questioned and specimen documents/ writings. when the issue is one of establishing the identity of the writer of a document or the genuineness of it, the expert will need to compare it with other documents of known origin. documentary evidence is very much important to prove the offence against the accused. admitted writings are those written by a person in his/her routine course of activity/business, which are freely written, natural. such writings are normally available in case of employees, students, working class people, professionals, business men etc. the admitted standard material can be procured in the following forms of documents. - (i) personal correspondence: post cards, applications for employment/loans/diaries/account books etc. - (ii) academic records: school/college/applications, answer scripts, notes, records, project reports etc. - (iii) financial and business documents: passed bank cheques, applications for demand drafts, withdrawal forms, account opening forms, specimen signature cards, ledgers, receipts etc. - (iv) vocational & professional documents: official correspondence, leave letters, applications for loans, attendance register, legal documents, etc. the most suitable admitted standards are those which belong to the same general class (post-card with post-card) and contemporary (near about date/month/year) to the questioned documents. any object that contains handwritten, typewritten or printer marking whose source or authenticity is in doubt is referred to as questioned document. all questioned documents involved in a particular investigation should be submitted to the forensic science laboratory for examination. questioned documents are identified by comparison of similarities. sample writings a especially written by the suspect for the purpose of comparison with the disputed documents. this is usually done either in the open court or in the presence of presiding officer. for collection of such specimens, there are some essential points to be followed to facilitate the experts' examination meaningful, objective and conclusive. - (i) the suspect should be seated in a comfortable posture to ensure comfortable and natural writing conditions. - (ii) he/she should be supplied similar paper and writing instruments (pen/ball pen) as that of the questioned document. - (iii) the suspect should not be shown the questioned document - (iv) dictation should be given to the suspect at different intervals to prevent the suspect from imitating the same style. - (v) after each dictation, the sheet of specimens should be removed, and a fresh sheet of paper to be supplied. - (vi) the suspect should be encouraged to write the bits and pieces in the questioned sample several times. - (vii) at least 3 specimens should be taken. in case of deliberate efforts to disguise, more specimens should be taken. - (viii) each and every specimen sheet should be attested by the investigating officer along with the signatures of the witness with date. forgery is an attempt made by a person to defraud another by falsely making altering a signature or any part of document. forgery is the process of making, adapting, or imitating objects, statistics, or documents with the intent to deceive for the sake of altering the public perception, or to earn profit by selling the forged item. the letter written by the person just before committing suicide is called suicide note. the investigating officer has to search for the suicide note at the scene. suicide notes are generally found in note books, pooja room, diaries and on plain papers. suicide notes are helpful in establishing the motive behind the suicide-if any person is responsible for the death, to know the state of mind of the person just before committing suicide. sometimes a fake suicide note may be planted to show a homicide as a suicide. care should be taken to check the hand writing of the deceased. the suicide note should be sent to fsl for handwriting analysis along with the old handwritings like notebooks and official correspondence, investigating officer should search the scene thoroughly for suicide notes.

page_content: whenever any officer in charge of police station or investigation officer considers that the production of any document or other thing is necessary for the purpose of investigation he may issue a written requisition to the person in whose possession or power such documents is believed to be requiring him to attend and produce it at a time and place stated in the order. document should be seized after conducting a search of a place where such document is kept. the instructions contained in the posts and telegraph manual, 1950 regarding production of records in the custody of the post office are given below for information of police officers making investigation of cases involving such records. records of a post office or mail office should be produced, and information available in them should be given on the written order of any police officer who is making an investigation under the cr.p.c., but only those entries in the records which relate to the person or persons charged with the offence under investigation or which are relevant to that offence should be disclosed. in any other case, the official in charge of the office should, without delay, refer for orders to the head of the circle, who will decide whether or not, under section 12 of the indian evidence act (1 of 1987), the information asked for should be withheld. when the information asked for by a police is not available in the records of the office concerned, the police or the excise officer should be informed accordingly, irrespective of the question whether the information, if available, might or might not be given'. if any document, parcel or thing in the custody of a postal or telegraph authority is required for the purpose of any investigation, the district magistrate, chief judicial magistrate, court of sessions or high court may require the postal or telegraph authority as the case may be to deliver the document to such person (s. 92 cr.p.c). investigating police officers will be given all facilities to inspect within the respective offices the original documents in the custody of postal offices. they will also have facilities to take out copies of such documents (including photostat copies). even where an original document has to be shown to a witness during the process of investigation, it will be possible in many cases to have that carried out at the postal office. the above instructions apply to documents, in the possession of the posts and telegraphs department. police officers are strictly forbidden to resort to the use of sections 91 and 92 of the code of criminal procedure to secure original documents from postal offices. ------- (related provisions of protection of crime scene are mentioned in appm order no.418, 419) - sho shall, by quickest available means, proceed to the scene of offence or depute staff with scene cordoning material like i.e. barricades, tapes for cordoning off etc. - a register should be kept at the entry point and names of the officers entered with date and time (and exit date and time also)should be invariably mentioned - this helps in elimination process if required - intruders - stray animals - neighbours - friends - sympathisers - curious onlookers - newspaper reporters - press photographers - others who are not officially connected. - provide information - investigative leads and other pertinent details - should be segregated and examined for collecting all possible information - eye-witnesses should be requested and allowed to stay at earmarked place for examination. - even if visible, it may escape a non-observant eye or may not appear relevant. - it is therefore necessary that the scene or places be subjected to close scrutiny both by experts (clues team) and the investigating staff. if the outdoor scene is busy place take steps to divert the traffic in alternative route.

page_content: indoor scene is easy to preserve when compared to outdoor scene. golden rule of soc is 'don't touch anything before taking photographs of soc. (related provisions of recording of crime scene by photography and videography are mentioned in appm order no.420) as per section 9 of the ie act, photographic evidence is admissible to explain or introduce a fact. the police have a limited time and opportunity to make an in-depth study of the crime scene in an undisturbed state. digital photographic cameras currently being used by clues teams facilitate quick reviews and recall of photographs on the screen and - before commencing the work, the whole area should be inspected and all the shots required should be carefully planned. - while planning the number of shots, ensure that enough overlapping shots are included to sequentialise the scene. - initially sufficient number of shots should be taken to give an overall perspective of the scene of crime, approach to the place, entry, access and exit points. - while photographing evidentiary items, indoors or outdoors, enough close-up shots should be taken to bring out interrelationships and to capture minute details. it is better to have three views taken viz.; - overall view, - mid-range view, and - close-up view of all important items of evidence - the chance fingerprints, footprints, etc. left at the scene of crime, should always be exposed from a close distance. - a scale or foot rule should be placed beside evidentiary items such as footprints, knife, injuries, cut marks, etc. to indicate the actual size in the enlarged photograph. - it is worthwhile to include separate numbered cards or plates for each evidentiary item so as to ensure proper identification at a later stage. - name of the photographer and address. - date and time each photograph was taken. - distance maintained between the lens and the object and direction. - technical information such as type of camera, focal length of lens, speed of the lens, effective aperture, shutter speed, film speed, type of flash. similar information should also be maintained for digital cameras. - special techniques such as oblique light, flood lamps, flashlight, uv light, poly light, filters etc., if used, should be noted against each shot. - light and weather conditions in cases of outdoor shots. - (a) the process of video documentation is similar to photography. - (b) in a burglary case for example the videography should start at the - (c) actual/suspected point of entry, - (d) cover the crime scene, - (e) point of exit and line of retreat. - (f) all-important spots can be focused through a zoom lens and more time can be allotted to cover from all angles - (g) all the precautions suggested earlier for photography should also be followed for these recordings. (related provisions of preparation of the sketch and method of sketching scene are mentioned in appm order no. 421 ) one of the methods of crime scene recording is preparation of the sketch of the scene in addition to other methods like photography, observation notes, videography and audio tape recording. note: a hb by night case sketch of the crime scene shall be prepared by the trainees by providing white paper and creating a crime scene. note: demo by dog squad in a murder case, a simulated crime scene shoud be created for the demo and shown to the trainees. on receipt of information about occurrence of crime sho shall, by quickest available means call for the experts informing on phone followed by requisition some of the experts are - mobile scientific investigation unit (appm order 554) - mobile clues team (appm order 554-4) - finger print expert - explosive expert (bd team) - medical officer to conduct pme at the scene in certain cases

page_content: - dog squad - motor vehicle inspector in accident cases appm order 554-5: requisition of clues team mandatory in certain types of cases it is mandatory for the investigating officers to seek the assistance of the clues teams in cases involving/relating to - (a) explosion - (b) firearm - (c) arson - (d) dowry death - (e) homicide - (f) property offence - (g) mass disaster - (h) road/train accident - (i) custodial death - (j) use of force by police - (k) industrial accident and any other sensational crime failure to requisition the services of the clues teams in the above types of cases will entail an appropriate disciplinary action. the services of experts should be utilized depending upon the nature of case, where ever their service is essential for the purpose of investigation. the requisition to expert should contain in addition to the general formalities of letter. - the brief facts of case. - the necessity of their service in that particular case. - nature of service you require so that they will bring related material. - the place and time you require their presence. evidence left behind by the criminal while committing the crime will be invariably present in all scenes of crime is based on - the principle of exchange propounded by french scientist professor edmund locard in 1928, which says' whenever any two objects or materials, animate or inanimate, come into contact with each other, - there is always transference of material from each object on to the other'. - it is therefore the primary responsibility of investigating officers to search, identify and collect such evidence independently or with the assistance of 'clues' team. (related provisions of preservation of physical and biological evidences are mentioned in appm order no.425) preservation of physical & biological evidences precautions to be taken to preserve such evidence - if the stain is on a solid object that can be moved, such as a knife, crowbar, fire arm or any other weapon, it is preferable to transport the whole object, protecting the area of the stain or completely enclosing the object in a package (if it is small enough). - food and edible substances should be placed in as many separate moisture proof bottles or containers as necessary to avoid any contamination. in case of suspected food poisoning the packages should be marked clearly as suspected or known samples of poison. - each item should be packed separately so as to avoid breakage, loss in transit - tweezers, forceps and similar tools are used to collect and place traces and small items into containers. it is advisable to use rubber gloves to handle such physical evidence. - an 'evidence box' such as deal wood box, clean empty cartons, cardboard boxes, etc. should be utilised for transporting the items of evidence. - items of evidence that need comparative analysis for possible commonness of origin should be packed in separate containers avoid any allegation of cross contamination - thoroughly clean and dry containers, wrapping paper, corrugated paper boxes and sealing tape are the basic safeguards for physical evidence in transport. appropriate containers such as glass bottles with a glass lid should be used for transporting substances such as acids. - if the stain is on clothing, the garments should be wrapped separately in paper, marked appropriately and packed. garments and other clothing such as bed sheets, towels, tablecloths and similar evidence should be folded as small as possible and without pressure. the specific areas of such fabric which contain trace evidence should be folded in such a way as to protect such areas to avoid loss of such materials by falling off, peeling off, etc. - it is preferable to send a soil-stained or mud-soaked object to the laboratory rather than to attempt to remove and send the soil or mud as separate items. - bed sheets, carpets, woollen materials from the scene of arson may be wrapped in metal foil and sealed in airtight containers.
