page_content: - pills and other non-caustic substances should be left in their original containers for transport to the laboratory. such containers often contain useful information. - documentary evidence should be first placed in transparent envelopes without folding or bending, and then placed between two pieces of rigid cardboard and packed in a suitable cloth-lined envelope cover. - plastic pill bottles such as used medicine containers with lids are unbreakable and can be easily sealed with tape. they make excellent containers for hair, fibres, and other small trace evidence. they are ideal for spent bullets, empty cartridge cases, and cartridges because they can be packed with absorbent cotton to minimise movement. plastic envelopes and bags are suitable for packing many materials. - trace evidence, such as hair, fibers, etc. should be sealed in folded paper or placed in clean, sealed envelopes. (related provisions of lifting of finger prints, foot prints, portrait parleyare mentioned in appm order no.766, 79) - always take care of the scene of crime articles which are exposed bearing chance prints by providing shelter or cover to protect them from sun, rain, dust etc., to prevent damage to the clues, before they are examined by an fp expert. - always make it a point to utilize the services of a fingerprint expert if any chance prints are suspected to exist on any article at the scene of crime. - when chance prints are found in narrow nooks and corners, or the inner surfaces of jewel cases, jars and flower vases, it is not possible to photograph them. - the finger prints should be secured and preserved by the process of lifting. - the method adopted in shifting fps is to press the gummy surface of a transparent cellophane tape against the developed chance prints without forming air bubbles lift it and transfer them to a contrast lift cards. - lifting should be resorted to only for those prints where photography is not possible, as there is every risk of the prints getting partly or wholly damaged in the process. - lifting of chance prints also can be done on all surfaces after photography, - making use of latest methods like iodine fuming, ninhydrin, glueing and applying multiple wave length light derived from polilight, poliray etc. or laser beams. - remember always to use separate containers for preserving severed fingers of an unidentified dead body. the methods commonly used for collecting footprints are: by preserving the original impression intact: if the foot print is found on a portable object such as a mat, slab, plank, card board, etc. it is convenient to carry the object bearing the print to the laboratory for photography and further analysis - it is advisable to take the photograph of the print even in such cases before removing them as there is always the risk of the original impression being damaged in packing or transit. - at least three photographs are to be taken, one direct view from the top and the other two from either side at an angle of 45 . - before taking the photograph place an identification slip or card (containing crime number, section of law, date, police station, district etc. with initials of the io) close to one side of the print and a foot rule on the other side to record the actual size of the footprint. tracing: tracing is a rough method to record surface prints. a glass (or preferably a celluloid sheet) is held over the print as close as possible. care should be taken to ensure that it does not come in contact with the print. then a sketch pen draws the contours along the print line. details such as the characters of the toes, ball of the foot, instep and heel together with crease; cracks, warts, etc. should carefully be traced on the sheet. - after the tracing is complete the sheet is removed and all the particulars of the case should be written on the glass together with the signature of the investigating officer and the witnesses. - on the basis of such prints on glass any number of tracings can be obtained on tracing paper. - the glass sheet and a tracing paper should be packed in between two cardboards for transport to the court. lifting: when the print is formed on a surface that is of a similar colour to the print, for instance, a blood stained print formed on a red surface, dusty (white) print formed on a white surface, dark (black) print formed on a black surface, - it becomes difficult to photograph the print because of the similar colour of the background. - footprints can also be lifted using photographic film or transparent adhesive tape. the fixed photographic film is moistened in water for few minutes until the emulsion side becomes tacky and then placed over the print with the sensitive side in contact with the print.

page_content: a uniform pressure is applied over the film for few seconds and it is then lifted carefully. the print is transferred on to the film, which can then be used as a negative to make copies from. casting: casts are prepared from foot impressions (sunken foot prints), tyre impressions or other impressions left behind by the criminal. a variety of materials are used to prepare the cast, e.g., plaster of paris, wax, resins, plastic powders, modelling clay, sulphur, lead, wood metal, plasticine, moulage material, silicone, etc. portrait parle is french term referring to a picture of a suspect in both front and profile views. modernization has led this to be our 'mug shot' of a suspect. we still use front and profile views but we have added in written descriptions of the suspect as well. we use portrait parles for finding suspects and missing persons because of the thoroughness of the descriptions. key descriptions for portrait parleys are as follows. notice that the descriptions essentially profile a person's whole personality, not just a physical descriptor. chin, voice = build. chin, friends = glasses. chin, hangouts = foot size. chin, piercings = residence. chin, hair length = hair texture. dna, voice = blood. dna, friends = habits. dna, hangouts = eye shape. dna, piercings = hair color. dna, hair length = exits scene. ears, voice = teeth. ears, friends = hobbies. ears, hangouts = lip color. ears, piercings = occupation. ears, hair length = accessories. head, voice = height. head, friends = jewelry. head, hangouts = eye color. head, piercings = background. head, hair length = fingerprints. name, voice = family. name, friends = lip size. name, hangouts = eyebrows. name, piercings = automobile. name, hair length = pecularities. neck, voice = gender. neck, friends = tattoos. neck, hangouts = lip shape. neck, piercings = facial hair. neck, hair length = weapon choice. nose, voice = shoes. nose, friends = weight. nose, hangouts = forehead. nose, piercings = ethnicity. nose, hair length = complexion. race, voice = scars. race, friends = . race, hangouts = clothing. race, piercings = lip prints. race, hair length = birthmarks just like automated fingerprint identification, facial recognition helps police to manage the records of people of interest. among other tasks, it provides an alternate, or additional, method to make sure that databases do not contain multiple records for a single individual. while this task is routinely performed by police agencies using fingerprints, facial recognition provides benefits when fingerprint data either does not exist or is not easily shared between agencies: the software available with police allows people to be identified, current automated face extraction techniques work well with almost full-frontal views of faces. the various parts of face/body of different types are shown in computer software to the victims of offence to identify the accused. some body parts are given below the facial features and if required figure of accused are drawn basing on the version of victim which is helpful to trace the accused. chin, facial hair = ethnicity. chin, weight = glasses. chin, page_content: the part of earth's surface that is not covered by water. - dry/wet land - agriculture/non-agriculture land - government land - private land - patta land - inam land - wakf board land, - temple lands (endowment land) - bhoodan land - forest land - ceiling and surplus land - urban/built up land land dispute involves conflicting claims to rights in land by two or more parties, focused on a particular piece of land, which can be addressed within the existing legal frameworks. land dispute may, or may not reflect some border conflict over land. however, we cannot define a land dispute in a single statement. each scenario is different. - (1) a person enters or encroaches upon property which is in the possession of others, with the intent to commit an offence, intimidate, insult, or annoy any person in possession of such property. - (2) a person entering the property and dispossessing those who are already in possession of such property. - (3) a person encroaches upon the neighboring land, by damaging/ removing the boundaries. - (4) a person encroaches upon the land belonging to others and takes up construction activity. - (5) a person trespasses into other's property and damages the existing boundaries like fencing, boundary stones and wall and threatens the persons who are safeguarding (watchman, security guards, tenants) in the absence of the landlords. - (6) a person trespasses into other's land and damages its nature, or demolishes the existing structures. - (7) a person enters other's property and hurts, threatens, and annoys a person who is already in possession of that land/property. - (8) a person, or a group, does unlawful activity of grabbing government land, a religious of charitable institution or endowment, including wakf or any other land, either by force, threat, or inducement with the criminal intent to take unauthorised possession of such property. the complainant should be a competent authority, i.e, a member of revenue, wakf board, forest department, endowment, department or any other government/ quasi-government agency. - (9) encroaching upon the ftl (full tank level) land, with criminal intent. creation of documents, where there is no land physically. - (1) related to the revenue department : title deeds, pasbooks, land/ house allotment certificate, mutation certificates, ror, pahanis, survey report/panchanamas, land conversion certificates, and ordrs of the rd, joint collector, and district collector. - (2) related to panchayat raj department: gp layouts, gp resolutions, approval of layouts by the panchayat raj officer, and reports of the panchyat secretary. - (3) related to the ghmc/hmda : property tax, building permission, layout, water tap connection bills, voter id card, ration card, aadhaar card, assessment, occupancy certificate, building penalizaion scheme final order, property valuation reports, mortgage and mortgage release orders, death certificates, lrs (land regulatizaion scheme). - (4) related to the office of the sub registrar : impersonation (instead of original owners, a fake/fictitious person executes the documents, personating himself as the original owner), validation to time expired unregistered document, rectification documents, false registered sale deed, false registered gpa, creating a false document taking advantage of missing volumes/records in sro office. - (5) related to the judicial court: court decrees, perpetual injunctions, interim injunctions, stay orders, judgments, status quos, and order of cancellation of registered documents. - (6) related to the electricity department: service connection documents, electricity bills. - (7) related to irrigation department: non ftl (full tank level) land certificate, flood embankment, drainage design, kuntas, reservoirs, tanks, anicuts, canals, their distributaries.

(1) multiple sale deeds, after taking sale consideration - (2) executing agreements/ sale deeds, without any right over the property - (3) executing agreements/sale deeds showing excess land (intentionally) - (4) executing agreements/sale deeds in an unauthrised manner (no right over the property), without having a supporting link document - (5) selling the land which was already acquired (in the process of acquisition) by government agencies, after receiving the compensation - (6) physical possession of the land differs from the survey number on the document. - (7) selling open land earmarked for community amenities/social infrastructures after releasing the mortgages from hmda/ghmc. - (8) executing sale agreement/sale deed in respect of mortgaged plot/ flat with hmda/ghmc and local bodies (licensing authority) before the release of mortgage by the competent authority. - (9) pre-launching sale transactions, i.e, before getting permissions from competent authorities, not acquiring required lands, the vendors go for sale of properties in the market through wide publicity and disappear after swindling the money. - (10) obtaining multiple loans on the property from various banks. - (11) obtaining loans from banks by inflating the price of property with the help of property valuators. - (12) obtaining loans from banks, showing excess physical area, and - (13) unregistered sale deeds/agreement of sale with regards to lands related to inam, laoni and assigned categories. knowingly selling the mortgaged lands without obtaining noc. whoever with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public, or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof destroys, or diminishes, its value or utility, or affects it injuriously. - (1) forging thumb impressions - (2) forging signatures - (3) counterfeit seals/stamps - (4) post-dated documents forgery by way of tampering with original documents (adding/erasing facts and figures) - (1) changing the content in the original document, and - (2) fraudulently adding or erasing the content/facts/figures/dates in the original documents. note: if a complaint is lodged by any authorized person/competent authority of any government/public agency with regard to land/property, the police officer should invariably register a case and investigate the matter. - (1) the complainant is unable to reveal prima-facie, i.e forgery, dispossession, trespass, damage to property, fake document, impersonation, criminal intimidation, or physical assault - (2) on the strength of an injunction order, an individual seeks dispossession of persons who are already in possession of the property. - (3) on the strength of valid sale deed, an individual seeks dispossession of persons who are already in possession of the property. - (4) family land/property disputesclaiming land without valid inheritance partition deeds, family gift deeds, and genuine will deeds. - (5) civil matters pending in the court with regard to title declaration: appeals, for any specific directions. - (6) tenant defaulting on rental payments. - (7) tenant subleasing the property to others. - (8) tenant/landlord violates the lease agreements and - (9) boundary and passage disputes. a person may acquire immobile property in any of the following ways: - (1) through will - (2) by way of purchase, etc. - (3) through gift, trust, settlement deeds - (4) thorough grant, sanad/inam by the government - (5) through partition deed, and - (6) through a court decree

page_content: - verbal contract: both the seller and the buyer can have a verbal agreement over the purchase or sale, of the land. - preparation of contract: once the contract is verbally agreed upon, it needs to be recorded on paper. this is done by preparing the agreement and sale document. - pay stamp duty: once the deed is made, you are required to pay the stamp duty; the stamp duty value may range from 3-7 percent. - execute and register sale deed: the next step is to execute the sale deed and register the same by visiting the sub-registrar's office. - property mutations: the final step is getting the mutation of the property done in the land and revenue records. this can be done by visiting the ghmc/ municipal authorities. the police officer should have - a thorough knowledge of the related acts/rules. - ability to understand the core issue/subject matter. - procedural fluency. - ability to critically scrutinize the available documents. - patience to listen to the claims of either party. - conceptual understating (connecting the links) - the ability to be judicious, impartial, and neutral - independent thinking and consciousness. - willingness to take legal advice. - the talent for taking actions within the ambit of law (police protection or aid) - the ability to be uninfluenced (politically or bureaucratically) - the discretion of not misusing one's official position for pecuniary benefits. - the fair-mindedness to not evict physical possession, and - the ability for collecting relevant evidence (oral/documentary) for successful disposal of the case. - (1) possession of the property denotes almost 90% of the rights to the possessor. - (2) persons having 2. (a) possession 3. (b) title 4. (c) records - (3) in the present scenario, only 10% of the people fulfill all these three categories. - (1) land address: i.e survey number, extent, boundaries, mandal. in a number of in stances, the survey number of the landowners differs from what is specified in the records. records are available, but physical land is not there. in order to establish the exact address of the land, verify the settlement/setwar document, khasra pahani and subsequent pahanis and their extent, visit the field and find out the survey number. - (2) who is owner of the land? the right way is to look at ror or ib, not the pass book, because a passbook is the extract for ror/dharani (sethwar, khasra or seshala). then establish the link between ror and setwar. ec and pahanies will help in finding out owner. if all are matching, the title is strong, if not, wrong. - (3) classification of land: i.e government land, forest land, ceiling, bhoodhan, assignment. these details are available in pahani, but there is every chance of mistakes in it. see the setwar for classification of land, because the setwar prevails over the pahani. the authority to change the classification vests with the joint collector. classification can be changed in the pahani through his order. - (4) who is in possession of the land? the cultivator column in the pahani clarifies it, but the present pahanis are not having the cultivation column (after dharani implementation). now the owner is presumed to be possessor of the land. physical verification will resolve it. - (5) find out what is the land problem: identity the nature of the land or property dispute. - (6) which act/law is applicable for the problem? - (7) which is the appropriate forum to address the issue? 1. police never evict the possessor. whoever is in factual/physical possession, it is the bounden duty of the police to see that the possession should not be disturbed. 2. police can intervene when there could be a breach of the peace by initiating 145, 107 crpc proceeding by sending a report to the executive magistrate. 3. comply with the court orders for police protection or aid. can be the absolute owners of the property

any authorized person deputed by the court. 6. the police have no rights to declare the title/ownership of the property. 7. any illegal occupants should be evicted through court orders only. 8. protection to be given at the time of survey to the concerned authorities on their written request. 9. no protection should be given for private survey. 10. even if a civil dispute is pending in any court, a criminal wrong may be investigated and the case should be finalized on its merits, but not to close as 'civil nature' without investigation. for this practical (simulation) exercise a mock case file is developed. the details of present case are a piece of land measuring 500 sq.yds. in survey no. 100 in rajendranager (v) allotted by the govt. to freedom fighter nadipalli narayan rao s/o krishna rao, age 70 years r/o h-no. 1-2-145 rajendranager (v) long back, but the land borders were not clearly demarcated while handing over allotment document to him. due to old age the said freedom fighter did not pursue the matter with the concerned authorities about the demarcation of plot. a local farmer akula srinivas s/o ramaiah age 45 years h. no. 1-3-150, rajendranager (v) who is having land adjacent to the land allotted to freedom fighter nadipalli narayan rao, has been claiming the part of said land and encroaching in to the land by ploughing the land and creating trouble to freedom fighter. the complainant has informed the issue to the sho over telephone: procedure to be followed by the sho in the above case: 1. the sho has to make a gd entry 2. he has to visit the scene on receipt of telephonic information of likelihood of breach of peace, took members of both the sides into custody u/s 151 cr.p.c. as they were designing to commit a cognizable offence likely to disturb peace and public tranquility. 3. a report has to be prepared and sent to the sub-divisional executive magistrate rajendranager u/s 145 cr.p.c. for taking further action as per the procedure. 4. another letter is to be prepared and sent to the asst. director, survey and land records, with request to depute a surveyor to do survey of the disputed land and to furnish report for taking further action. 5. the respondents taken in to custody were released on bail since both the parties agreed to file their representations before the subdivisional executive magistrate rajendranager for settlement of their border dispute. 6. after receiving the survey report from the ad, survey and land records, basing on the findings the sho has to take legal action. arrest to prevent the commission of cognizable offences 1. a police officer knowing of a design to commit any cognizable offence may arrest, without orders from a magistrate and without a warrant, the person so designing, if it appears to such officer that the commission of the offence cannot be otherwise prevented. whenever an executive magistrate is satisfied from a report of a police officer or upon other information that a dispute likely to cause a breach of the peace exists concerning any land or water or the boundaries thereof, within his local jurisdiction, he shall make an order in writing, stating the grounds of his being so satisfied, and requiring the parties concerned in such dispute to attend his court in person or by pleader, on a specified date and time, and to put in written statements of their respective claims as respects the fact of actual possession of the subject of dispute. in the court of the sub-divisional magistrate, rajendra nagar, ranga reddy district. sir, the state represented (a) the search shall be made in the presence of witnesses. 4. (b) list of articles seized and the places where they are found is to be prepared. 5. (c) the list is to be prepared by the officer and signed by witnesses. 6. (d) the persons witnessing the search shall not be summoned to attend the court as witnesses for the search unless specifically summoned by it. 2. the occupant or owner shall be allowed to be present and permitted to attend. a copy of the list prepared of the articles seized must be delivered to him. 3. whenever a search is made of a person and any article seized from searched person, a list of articles seized must be delivered to him. 4. a person when called for refuses or neglects to attend and witness a search under section his liable under section 187 cr.p.c. whenever a person is arrested by a police officer making the arrest may search such person and place in safe custody all articles, other than his clothes. any article seized from the person shall be listed and the list shall be furnished to the arrested person. whenever a female is to be searched, the search shall be made by another female with strict regard to decency. if any incriminating material or weapons or found on the arrested person they shall be seized and delivered to the court for forwarding to analysis by fsl. (sec.52 c.rp.c) 1. any magistrate can issue a warrant except for items in the post and telegrams. a magistrate may issue a warrant under this section even if there is no proceeding pending in his court. 2. a warrant may be issued when there is reason to believe that the articles or documents cannot be produced by a summons. it can be issued when it is not certain that these are with the particular person. or when the court considers that the enquiry, trial or other proceedings will be served by a general search or inspection. 3. the warrant can be issued to any person including the police officer. 4. a warrant can be in general terms or may be restricted to a particular 5. place of search. 5. for the search of a document, parcel or article in the custody of postal or telegraphs authority only the district magistrate or the chief judicial magistrate is competent to issue a warrant. 1. whenever a magistrate has reason to believe that a place is used for 2. the deposit or sale of stolen property or objectionable article, he may issue a warrant to enter, to search, to take possession of such an article, to convey such property before a magistrate or guard it, to take into custody every person suspected to be involved. objectionable articles in this section include counterfeit coins, counterfeit currency, forged document, false seals, and obscene objects. 2. instruments used for production of the above. 1. the warrant may be issued to the police officer not below the rank of s.i. to enter any premises and search. 2. the warrant may be issued for the seizure of any news paper, book or document. a warrant may be issued if the state government declares by notifications specifying the reasons that the matter is punishable under sections 124 (a), 153 (a), 153 (b), 292, 293 and 295 (a) ipc and opines that the documents or book or news papers should be forfeited. documents include any painting, drawing or photograph or other visible representation. in case of emergency, the police officer can invoke section 165 cr.p.c and make searches 3. and seizures without obtaining a warrant from the magistrate. if any district magistrate, sub-divisional magistrate or magistrate of the first class has reason to believe that any person is confined under such circumstances that the confinement amounts to an offence, he may issue a search-warrant, and the person to whom such warrant is directed may search for the person so confined; and such search shall be made in accordance therewith, and the person, if found, shall be immediately taken before a magistrate, who shall make such order as in the circumstances of the case seems proper.

page_content: search memo and search list: a list of all articles shall be prepared and a copy furnished to the owner of the house. the following are the contents of the search list. 1. place 2. date and time 3. under which order search is made 4. details of the officer who conducted the search 5. list of articles found 6. signature of the person / owner / resident 7. signatures of the witnesses 8. signature of the io / officer seizure means the act of taking the physical possession of property. a search is made for seizure. they are closely connected. search is made by a police officer to prove the occurrence of a crime. seizure not only helps in the detection of the case but is useful as evidence including the charge sheet against the accused. seizure is made - at the scene of crime - from witnesses - in search of various places of accused and - on the confession made by the accused, from his possession or at his instance. - every seizure is made in the presence of witnesses and record (panchanama) is made. - according to section 47 cr.p.c, if the accused has hidden in some place it can be searched and accused shall be arrested. - according to section 51 cr.p.c, whatever is found on the accused except his clothing shall be seized. a list of articles seized shall be prepared and receipt given to him. - u/s 52 cr.p.c the officer can seize any offensive weapons found on the arrested person. - any police officer may seize any property which may be suspected to be stolen u/s 102 cr.p.c. form 60: depositing of property to the court: procedure for disposal of perishable property/drugs: 1. when ever any perishable articles are found during the search, they have to be seized under the cover of panchanama, in the presence of independent witnesses. the details are to be given to the executive magistrate/judicial magistrate who in turn disposes them after conducting an auction. the proceedings of the auction are to be submitted to the court having jurisdiction of the area. in case, drugs are found they have to be seized under the cover of panchanama in the presence of independent witnesses and information sent to the controller of drugs and the district collector to take necessary action and disposal. ------

page_content: case diaries are maintained as per the provisions of section 172 cr.p.c. every police officer making an investigation under this chapter shall day by day enter his proceedings of the investigation in a diary, setting forth the time at which the information reached him, the time at which he began and closed his investigation, the place or places visited by him, and a statement of the circumstances ascertained through his investigation. case diaries are the records of the entire investigation conducted by the investigating officers and different types of evidence gathered. it will be helpful to record the entire course of investigation. even if the investigation officer is transferred the case diary helps in the continuation of investigation. the case diary includes - the 161 statements of the witnesses - the copy of complaint - fir copy - rough sketch of the scene of offence - the material object seized - the letter of advice to fsl for analysis - description of wounds - requisition sent to the medical officer - wound certificate - post mortem examination certificate - and all relevant records useful for investigation. - the investigating officer and his superiors shall ensure the physical safety of the case diary as well as its contents. - the access to the case diaries is limited only to the io, the superior officers and the concerned legal officers who are in charge at a given time and not to others. - the officer who has custody of case diaries either as an io or a superior officer supervising investigation should ensure that it is handed over to their successors in office. - the first case diary should commence with a brief summary of the fir, the time of receipt of the complaint, delay if any, in starting - for the scene, the time of departure and arrival at the scene, and description and plan of the scene as prescribed in order 420 and 421 of chapter 22. - in case where there is no scene of offence like in financial crimes, the records and places where such records or data recording, storage or retrieval systems are located, the method of crime as reflected at such places, objects, computers, documents etc. and the plan or sketch or diagram representing the crime scene may be described or drawn up as is possible. - a statement of circumstances ascertained through the investigation at the place or places visited by the investigating officer, and the date and hour of closing the investigation shall be noted. - every step taken by the io shall be mentioned as concisely as possible. - every clue obtained, even if at the time appears likely to be of no value, houses searched with reasons for the search and the names of witnesses to the search are to be noted. - property recovered its description and place where it was found to be noted. - arrests, information obtained which is likely to prove of value, and methods adopted by the suspects/accused are among the things to be mentioned in the case diary. - the substance of the statements of witnesses shall find place in the case diary. - the io shall record in form 65 the statements of persons examined by him in detail separately and attach to the case diary of the day as part ii. - case diaries and statement of witnesses will be duplicated by carbon process, or by photocopiers. the original is retained in the station and the other sent to the sdpo concerned who shall deal with the case diaries in the manner prescribed - in the concluding diary, the investigating officer shall record a summary of the reasons which have guided his final decision in the case. if he considers that there is no case, his reasons will, of course, be more detailed and fuller than the one sent up for trial. - the names of informers need not be entered in the diary and no court can compel an investigating officer to disclose the name of an informer. - when a case is transferred from one investigating officer to another, the officer taking over the investigation shall take charge of the diary, noting therein the date of assuming charge of the investigation of the case. - those who, under the orders and directions of the investigating officer, assist him by making any enquiries do not thereby become

page_content: in january 2016 four constables were convicted for torturing khicchi and causing his death in police custody. in handing down guilty verdicts, judge sm bhosale noted, 'the word 'custody' implies guardianship and protective care. even when applied to indicate arrest or incarceration, it does not carry any sinister symptoms of violence during custody.' the four policemen were each sentenced to seven years in prison for culpable homicide not amounting to murder and for voluntarily causing hurt to extract a confession. a critical piece of evidence in this case was the postmortem report. khicchi's post-mortem report shows 56 injuries and final cause of death as 'head injury with multiple injuries.' according to the report, all contusions were caused by a hard and blunt object. when the police attributed those injuries to a mob attack, khicchi's family members urged the police commissioner to transfer the investigation to an independent agency. in october 2013, the crime branch of the state criminal investigation department took over the case. a month later, the crime branch filed charges and arrested four police officers on murder charges. . copy of post-mortem report of aniket khicchi dt. october 28, 2013 in khicchi's case, the court noted several procedural violations at the time of arrest. the arrest record was drawn up at 2.40 pm on october 26, but the policemen who took the two suspects into custody did not register a first information report or make an entry into the police station diary as required by law until after 8 pm. until that time, khicchi and vani were illegally detained and interrogated inside the police station. the court also said that even though the accused police officers claimed that khicchi was assaulted by a mob and that they had rescued him from about 60 to 70 people, the policemen did not take him for the required medical check-up. eyewitness, vani's testimony played an important role in ensuring the conviction of the accused police officials. where a person is arrested on a charge of committing an offence and his identification by any other person or persons is considered necessary for the purpose of investigation of such offence, the court, having jurisdiction, may on the request of the officer in charge of a police station, direct the person so arrested to subject himself to identification by any person or persons in such manner as the court may deem fit. test identification parade is useful in taking the investigation in the right direction. ti parade is to be corroborated with the other evidence (sec 157 iea). ti parade is conducted by judicial magistrate to identify the offender or lost property by the witness (sec.9 iea). ti parade is taken up only when the accused is in judicial custody. requisition has to be filed to chief judicial magistrate. the police officers make all arrangements and completely withdraw. the accused should be mingled with persons of similar height, weight, stature, age and status in proportion of minimum of 1:5 and maximum of 1:10. witnesses should be called in one by one to identify the accused. the identification of property takes place in the court only. each item of the property shall be put up separately for the parade. it shall be mixed up with four or five similar objects. before identification of the property the witness shall be asked the description of his property. the proceedings of identification of the property shall be drawn by the presiding officer. witnesses may be shown photographs of the suspect mixed with several non suspects and asked to identify. later regular identification parade should also be held. photographs should not be shown before t.i parade to witness. publication of the suspect photos in media is counterproductive. cctv footage should be seized by the io with the help of cyber crime experts and should not be published in the media. this is similar to property identification. the recovered weapons shall be mixed with similar weapons and asked to be identified by the witness. witness can be the expert also (sec.45 iea). under the identification of the prisoners act 1920, police can take the finger print, photograph and other measurements if a person is convicted of an offence punishable with one year or more or ordered to give security for good behavior under section 110 cr.p.c. refusal to give measurements will be punishable under section 186 ipc. identification of prisoners rules 1975 (rule 12 & 14) gives the classes of persons whose finger prints and photographs are taken. this information should be maintained in police station records, dcrb/scrb and ncrb. 1. arrest: police officer shall not touch the person of the woman for making her arrest unless the police officer is a female (sec.46 cr.p.c). arrest of woman should not be made after sun set and before sun rise. 2. search: a woman suspect can be searched only by another woman.

18.7.17 3. name of the accused: a-1 s. vinod kumar, s/o s. santosh kumar, age 20yrs, occ. 1 st year b.com, r/o. flat no. 417, navaratna towers, road no. 1, rajendranagar, rr. dist - a-2 md. shaukath, date: ................... honoured sir, the brief facts of the case are that on 17.7.17, complainant's daughter kum. poonam, aged 16yrs left their house at about 7pm informing him that she is going to attend a birth day party of her friend monica. till late in the night also when there was no response from poonam's cell, complainant lodged a report with rajendranagar ps at about 00:30 hrs that his daughter may have been kidnapped by unknown accused and asi sri krishna who was on duty at ps rajendranagar registered this complaint as in cr. no......601...../17 u/s 365 ipc and took up investigation. asi informed sho and other superior officers. on 18.7.17 at about 0900hrs on receipt of information that an unknown dead body is lying in the outskirts of himayatsagar, on north/west side, visited the said place along with complainant sanjay kumar, his wife sunita and they identified the dead body of girl found in bushes to be that of poonam their daughter who is aged 16 and suspected that poonam has been raped and murdered. an observation of scene of offence panchnama was conducted in the presence of panchwitnesses: 1. mohdmiyan, s/o. chandpasha, age 45years, occ. owner of cafeteria, r/o. 8-506, rajandranagar, rr distt. 2. md. sardar khan, s/o. md. hafeez khan, age 32yrs, occ. supervisor in suvidha plaza complex, r/o. 7-722, rajendranagar, rr. distt. the scene of offence was photographed and sketch drawn. an inquest over the dead body of poonam was held in the presence of panch witness: 1. shri p. ranga rao, s/o. anand rao, age 40 yrs, farmer, h.no. 4-76, himayatsagar village, rajendra nagar mandal 2. shri m. rohit, s/o. laxman, age 38 yrs, ward member, gram panchayat, h.no. 3-650, himayatsagar 3. smt. k. durga, w/o. shivareddy, 38 yrs, himayatsagar, social worker, h.no. 4-290, himayatsagar on18.7.17 during the inquest, examined parents of deceased sanjay kumar, sunita and ram reddy a neighbouring land owner and all of them unanimously opined that the death of poonam was apparently due to rape and murder. a memo was filed before this court adding sections 376, 302, 201 ipc r/w sec 5 of pocso act. the dead body was subjected to pm exam with a team of doctors including a lady doctor. spot opinion by asst prof forensic medicine sri narayan reddy is given where it is mentioned that poonam was raped and murdered by stab injuries over the breast and abdomen. during the course of examination, call details of cell phone used by deceased before going to party were obtained and tower location also taken at that time. in the light of these details, the io checked the cc footage of sbh, rajendranagar, which was given as tower location of the said cell on 17.7.17.

in the name of santosh kumar and also driving licence of vinok kumar and they were seized. reddish stains found in the dickey of the said car were also lifted, packed and sealed to send the same for forensic examination. on being led by accused a-1, at his instance, located a-2 md. shaukat at his home. a-2 shaukat's confession was also recorded in the presence of panch witness md. miya and sardar khan and in pursuance of the said confession at the instance of a-2, accompanied by panch witnesses as mentioned above, visited farm house near himayatsagar village and seized one hand bag with one cell phone inside, a pair of foot wear and also identity card and all the mo including bedsheets, reddish stains present on floor were seized in the presence of panchayatdars. later seized blood stained cloths of a1 and a2 from their respective house and also blood stained knife from a2's house. all the mos i.e blood stained cloths of a1 & a2, knife, tyre marks of the said car, were packed, sealed. both the accused a1 & a2 are forwarded to the professor forensic medicine, osmania medical college for examining them and draw specimens of blood, semen, saliva, urine and also for comparison of dna and also subjected to potency test. all the mos as mentioned are forwarded to fsl for evaluation and report along with specimens of blood, saliva, urine, semen etc of a1 & a2. in this case some more witnesses are to be examined in respect of digital, physical and documentary evidence and test identification parade by watchman has to be conducted, the accused a1 and a2 are herewith produced before this hon'ble court - under the escort of hc 1212, pcs 2222, 3210 with a prayer to remand the said accused for judicial custody for a period of 15 days so as to enable to complete investigation and file chargesheet. trainee investigating officer rajendranagar ps provisions of 167(2)cr.p.c: the magistrate to whom an accused person is forwarded under this section may, - whether he has or has not jurisdiction to try the case, from time to time, - authorise the detention of the accused in such custody as such magistrate thinks fit, for a term not exceeding fifteen days. as mentioned above any magistrate is authorised to remand the accused initially only for a period of 15 days. since this is a mandatory provision , - the io should file a requisition in the court on the day of expiry of remand period requesting the court to extend his remand period for a further period of 15 days, - likewise io has to file requisitions in the court on expiry of remand period till the accused gets bail. - otherwise there are chances for magistrate to release the accused on bail. page_content: the necessity of obtaining transit warrant arises, when the accused of a particular case is arrested by io in the place which is situated at a long distance from the place where jurisdictional magistrate court is situated. once an accused is arrested by io, he has power to keep him in his custody for 24 hours as per 167 cr.p.c. then io has to produce the accused in the court of nearest magistrate from the place of arrest and file a requisition mentioning the facts to issue transit warrant to permit him to take accused to his jurisdiction area and to produce him in the area jurisdiction court. in the court of judicial first class magistrate ____________ honored sir, sub: investigation of case of house burglary - issue of transit warrants against accused - request - regarding. ref: cr. no: _______ /2023 u/s 457, 380 ipc of ps ________. * * * it is submitted that on the intervening night of ___________ (date), some unknown offenders gained entrance into the house metropolitan magistrate court at rajendranager hyderabad. it is prayed that a transit warrant may be issued with permission to produce him in jurisdictional court at hyderabad after 3 days of transit period station house officer / ci ___________ ps / circle ------ the word 'bail' has not been defined in law. the meaning of the term 'bail' is to set free a person who is under arrest, by taking security for his appearance in court. in schedule-i cr.p.c, the offences cognizable, noncognizable; bailable and non-bailable have been mentioned. provisions as to bail and bonds are mentioned from section 436 to 450 cr.p.c. article -21 of the constitution of india states that, 'no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to the procedure established by law.' it is the right of the arrested/detained person in bailable offences to obtain bail. either the accused may be enlarged on bail by the sho or court on executing a personal bond. sub-section 2 of section 436 cr.p.c empowers the court to refuse bail to an accused person even if the offence is bailable where the person granted bail fails to comply with the conditions of the bail bond. deals with maximum period of detention of the under trial prisoner, where a person has undergone detention for a period extending up to one-half of the maximum period of imprisonment specified for that offence (not for the offence for which death punishment is specified), he shall be released by the court on his personal bond with or without sureties. it is done only after hearing the public prosecutor. in case of bailable offence, the grant of bail is a matter of right. it may be either given by a police officer who is having the custody of accused or by the court.the accused may be released on bail, on executing a bond, known as 'bail bond', with or without furnishing sureties. the 'bail bond' may contain certain terms and conditions, such as: - (a) the accused will not leave the territorial jurisdiction of the state without permission of court or police officer. - (b) the accused shall give his presence before police officer every time he is required to do so. - (c) the accused will not tamper with any evidence whatsoever, considered by police in the investigation. - (d) the court is empowered to refuse bail to an accused person even if the offence is bailable, where the person granted bail fails to comply with the conditions of the bail bond.

page_content: when bail can be taken in non-bailable offences: when any person accused of, or suspected of, the commission of any non-bailable offence is arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police station or appears or is brought before a court other than the high court or court of session, he may be released on bail, - (i) except the offences punishable with death or imprisonment for life - (ii) and such person who has previously been convicted of an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life or imprisonment for seven years or more provided that the court may also direct 'that a person referred to in clause (i) or clause (ii) be released on bail if such person is under the age of sixteen years or is a woman or is sick or infirm. a non-bailable offence is one in which the grant of bail is not a matter of right. here the accused will have to apply to the court, and it will be the discretion of the court to grant bail or not. the application for bail shall be filed before the magistrate, who is conducting the trial. the application after being filed is usually listed on the next day. on such day, the application will be heard, and the police shall also present the accused in court. the magistrate may pass such orders, as he thinks fit. if the bail is granted, the accused will have to execute a 'bail bond'. on execution of bail-bond the accused is out of prison only on such terms and conditions, as contained in the 'bail-bond'. the amount of every bond, i.e. the security shall be reasonable and not excessive (sec 440). if, at any point of time, the terms and conditions of bail are not fulfilled, the 'bond' shall be forfeited. the application for bail shall be made in the form, prescribed and the designation of judge/magistrate should be clearly mentioned. the application shall also contain an undertaking, that the accused shall fulfill all the conditions as contained in the bail- bond. in non-bailable cases after forwarding the accused to the court and on filing the bail petition by the accused in the court, the io has to file counter by opposing the bail petition filed by the court by mentioning the grounds. 1. the accused may commit further offence 2. the accused may threaten the witnesses 3. the accused may try to tamper with the evidence 4. the accused may flee away from the jurisdiction 5. the accused may not cooperate with the investigating agency 6. there is likelihood of breach of peace and tranquility in the locality 7. there is likelihood of retaliation by the victim party 8. the accused is required for police custody for recovery of the crime weapon. 9. the accused is required for police custody for recovery of stolen property. 10. the accused has to be sent to judicial custody for conducting of test identification parade. 11. if the accused is released on bail, the victim and the general public may loose confidence in the police as well as judiciary. directions for grant of bail to person apprehending arrest: - (1) where any person has reason to believe that he may be arrested on accusation of having committed a non-bailable offence, he may apply to the high court or the court of session for a direction under this section that in the event of such arrest he shall be released on bail. the court may, after taking into consideration the following factors: 2. (i) the nature and gravity of the accusation; 3. (ii) the antecedents of the applicant including the fact as to whether he has previously undergone imprisonment on conviction by a court in respect of any cognizable offence; 4. (iii) the possibility of the applicant to flee from justice; 5. (iv) where the accusation has been made with the object of injuring or humiliating the applicant by having him so arrested, either reject the application forthwith or issue an interim order for the grant of anticipatory bail. - (2) when the high court or the court of session makes a direction under sub-section (1), the in charge of the police station shall not arrest as long as he complies with the conditions laid down in it. in the court of shri ___________________________________________________ police station _________________ next date of hearing __________________ f.i.r. no. ______________ under section/s ______________________________ sent to jail on ______________. bail bond i, __________________ son/daughter of shri ____________________________, resident of ___________________________________________________________, having been arrested or detained without warrant by the officer incharge of _________________________________ police station for having been brought before this hon'ble court charged with the offences of ____________ _ ___________________ and request to give surety for my attendance before

power to seize a passport under section 102 cr.p.c. if it is permissible within the authority given under section 102 of cr.p.c., it does not have power to retain or impound the same, because that can only be done by the passport authority under section 10(3) of the passports act. thereafter the police must send it along with a letter to the passport authority clearly stating that the seized passport deserves to be impounded for one of the reasons mentioned in section 10(3) of the act. it is, thereafter, for the passport authority to decide whether to impound the passport or not. since impounding of a passport has civil consequences, it does not have power to retain or impound the same, because that can only be done by the passport authority under section 10(3) of the passports act. the passports act is a special law while the cr.p.c. is a general law. it is well settled that the special law prevails over the general law. hence, impounding of a passport cannot be done by the court under section 104 cr.p.c though it can impound any other document or thing. the passport authority must give an opportunity of hearing to the person concerned before impounding his passport. ( state of orissa vs binapani dei [air 1967 sc 1269) ------ please read related provision in this connection in section 169 of the code of criminal procedure. district: rangareddy circle..................station house model ps , tspa case no...... 1/2023 u/s 279,337 ipc & sec. 187 r/w 134 (a) (b) of the mv act date of bail bond 9-8-2023 name of the person (accused ) ganesh s/o shanker, age 30 years r/o rajendranager on whose account (name of surety) rama rao s/o dasharatah...age 40 years r/o h.no. 4-2-8 rajendranager..............................who stands security. to appear before viii metropolitan magistrate. at rajendranager charge...... u/s 279,337 ipc & sec. 187 r/w 134 (a) (b) of the mv act 1988 ........................amount of bond, rs......10000/-........................ i hereby declare myself (or, we jointly and severally declare ourselves and each of us) surety (or sureties) for the above said...ganesh s/o shanker... age 30 years r/o rajendranager .................. that he shall attend at....... at rajendranager.............................in the court of...... viii metropolitan magistrate........................or in any court to which the case may be transferred on the............whenever called................ day of.........................next (or, on such day as he may hereafter be required to attend), further to answer to charge pending against him, and in case of his making default therein i hereby bind myself (or we hereby bind ourselves) to forfeit to government the sum of rupeesrs......10000/-... page_content: district:ranga reddy circle.......................station house... model ps...tspa .................... case no...1/2023 u/s 279,337 ipc & sec. 187 r/w 134 (a) (b) of the mv act 1988 of model p.s tspa...............to appear before............ viii metropolitan magistrate..........at......rajendranager......................................witnesses charge... u/s 279,337 ipc & sec. 187 r/w 134 (a) (b) of the mv act ................... amount of bond rs....rs......10000/- (rupees ten thousands) i, ganesh s/o shanker...age 30years r/o ......... rajendranager........... being charged with the offence of... u/s 279,337 ipc & sec. 187 r/w 134 (a) (b) of the mv act ...............and after inquiry required to appear before the magistrate of......... viii metropolitan magistrate ...................(or) and after inquiry called upon to enter into my own recognizance to appear when required, do hereby bind myself to appear at...... whenever called............. in the court of...... viii metropolitan magistrate ...........................or in any court to which the case may be transferred on the day of ......... whenever called....................................next (or such day as i may hereafter be required to attend) to answer further to the said charge, and in case of my making default herein, i bind myself to forfeit to government the sum of rupees dated, this......9th................................day of......... aug................2023 sd/- sd/- witnesses (signature) please read related provision in this connection in section 169 of the code of criminal procedure the io should promptly attend court and brief the pp for opposing the bail clearly indicating the reasons for opposing the bail. the cd file to be updated as the court may like to peruse while hearing the bail petition. following are the some of the points for opposing bail (the reasons depend upon case). - the release of offenders might cause disappearance of further evidence. - after release he may go out of view and will not be available for further investigation and recoveries. - the offenders may be concerned in other cases and nbws are pending. - insist for local sureties in the event of granting bail. memo for cancellation of bail please read related provision in this connection in section 437 and 438 of the code of criminal procedure section 437(5) crpc: any court which has released a person on bail under subsection (1) or sub-section (2), may, if it considers it necessary so to do, direct that such person be arrested and commit him to custody. in the court of additional metropolitan sessions judge, hyderabad, sir, sub: submission of a report against the accused a1 and a2 concerned in cr. no. 33/2023 u/s 304 (b), 498 (a) ipc & dowry prohibition act. of the model ps for cancellation of bail- reg. - ref:cr. no. 33/2023 u/s 304 (b), 498 (a) ipc & dowry prohibition act. of the model ps. * * * it is established, from the facts and evidence collected during the course of investigation that the accused a-1 (husband of the deceased) and a-2 (mother-in-law of the deceased) subjected the deceased to harassment and cruelty in order to extract a car as an additional dowry and in furtherance of their unlawful demand and illegal motive, strangulated the deceased, doused her with kerosene and set her on fire causing her death. both the accused (a1 &a2) were arrested on 12-06-2023 u/s 304 (b), 498 (a) ipc & sec. 4 dowry prohibition act by the i.o. - (1) the accused (a1) & (a2) were produced at viii m.m. at rajendra nagar along with the remand report on 13-06-2023. both the accused were sent for judicial custody. they were kept in central prison, charlapalli. - (2) the accused (a1) & (a2) were granted bail on 30-06-2023 by the additional metropolitan sessions judge, hyderabad, with the following terms and conditions. the accused ---- - (a) shall co-operate with the io, in the matter of investigation of above mentioned case. - (b) shall not tamper with the evidence of eye witnesses / mediators - (c) shall not leave the twin cities of hyderabad and secundrabad without prior permission of the court
